records.ety: [F. occasion, L. occasio, fr. occidere, occasum, to fall down; ob (see Ob-) + cadere to fall. See Chance, and cf. Occident.]

defs: A falling out, happening, or coming to pass; hence, that which falls out or happens; occurrence; incident; event.
quotes: The unlooked-for incidents of family history, and its hidden excitements, and its arduous occasions.

defs: A favorable opportunity; a convenient or timely chance; convenience.
quotes: Sin, taking occasion by the commandment, deceived me.
I'll take the occasion which he gives to bring Him to his death.

defs: An occurrence or condition of affairs which brings with it some unlooked-for event; that which incidentally brings to pass an event, without being its efficient cause or sufficient reason; accidental or incidental cause.
quotes: Her beauty was the occasion of the war.

defs: Need; exigency; requirement; necessity; as, I have no occasion for firearms.
quotes: After we have served ourselves and our own occasions.
When my occasions took me into France.

defs: A reason or excuse; a motive; a persuasion.
quotes: Whose manner was, all passengers to stay, And entertain with her occasions sly.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Occasioned (k*kā"zhnd); p. pr. & vb. n. Occasioning.]

defs: To give occasion to; to cause; to produce; to induce; as, to occasion anxiety.
quotes: If we inquire what it is that occasions men to make several combinations of simple ideas into distinct modes.
